title: Quiz: Hartree-Fock Theory Basics
description: Test your understanding of Hartree-Fock theory fundamentals

questions.question: What is the mean-field approximation in Hartree-Fock theory?

options.text: Replacing instantaneous e-e repulsion with an average field
Using the mean of all possible wavefunctions

questions.explanation: The mean-field approximation replaces the instantaneous electron-electron repulsion (which couples all electrons) with an average potential. Each electron moves in the average field created by all other electrons.

questions.question: What mathematical form does the Hartree-Fock wavefunction take?

options.text: A linear combination of Slater determinants

questions.explanation: Hartree-Fock uses a single Slater determinant as the wavefunction ansatz. This automatically satisfies antisymmetry but misses electron correlation from multiple determinants.

questions.question: What is the exchange operator K̂?

options.text: A local operator representing classical repulsion
A non-local operator arising from antisymmetry

questions.explanation: The exchange operator K̂ is non-local (depends on the orbital everywhere, not just at point r) and arises purely from the antisymmetry requirement for fermions. It has no classical analog.

questions.question: What is the Fock operator?
questions.explanation: The Fock operator f̂ = ĥ + Σⱼ(Ĵⱼ - K̂ⱼ) includes the one-electron Hamiltonian (kinetic + nuclear) plus the mean-field electron-electron interactions (Coulomb minus Exchange).

questions.question: The Hartree-Fock equations can be solved directly without iteration because they are linear eigenvalue equations.
questions.explanation: False. Although the HF equations look like eigenvalue equations (f̂χᵢ = εᵢχᵢ), the Fock operator f̂ depends on the orbitals {χⱼ} we're solving for. This requires an iterative, self-consistent field (SCF) procedure.

questions.question: According to the variational principle, how does E_HF compare to E_exact?
questions.explanation: The variational principle guarantees that any approximate wavefunction gives an energy that is an upper bound to the exact energy: E_HF ≥ E_exact. The difference is the correlation energy.

questions.question: What is 'spin contamination' in UHF?

options.text: Mixing of up and down spin orbitals
The UHF wavefunction is not a pure spin eigenfunction
Too many electrons have the same spin

questions.explanation: In UHF, α and β electrons have different spatial orbitals. This lowers the energy but means the wavefunction is not a pure spin eigenfunction—it's contaminated by higher spin states.

questions.question: Which properties does Hartree-Fock typically get right? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: a) HF usually gives good equilibrium geometries. c) Dipole moments are often reasonable. However, HF fails for: b) bond dissociation (qualitatively wrong) and d) dispersion interactions (completely missing).
